title.en: Data Privacy, Training Opt-Outs & Enterprise Security

description.en: Master the questions security teams ask of every AI vendor — training on customer data, retention, residency, access controls — and encode a data-handling policy as an automated compliance checker.

story_variants.en:
## The Meeting Every AI Project Must Survive

At some point, every enterprise AI initiative lands in a conference room with the security and privacy team, and the questions are always the same four: **Does the vendor train on our data? How long do they keep it? Where does it live? Who can see it?** Projects with crisp answers proceed; projects with hand-waving stall for quarters. This lesson gives you the crisp answers — and, in the challenge, a way to *automate* asking them.

## Question 1: Training on Your Data

The headline fear ("our secrets will come out of the model for competitors") maps to a real but well-managed control: **training-data policy**. The market has converged on a clear pattern across Anthropic, OpenAI and Google alike:

- **Enterprise API and business-tier products: no training on your data by default.** This is contractual, not just a settings toggle.
- **Consumer tiers: different defaults**, varying by vendor and changing over time — users may need to opt out, and policy updates have repeatedly moved these lines. This asymmetry is *the* core argument for governed enterprise seats over shadow AI (as the ChatGPT rollout lesson argued).
- **Nuances that security reviewers will find if you don't**: abuse-monitoring and safety review can involve limited human access to flagged content under retention windows; opt-in feedback programs (thumbs-up/down with data sharing) can create training-eligible data even in business contexts; and fine-tuning services have their own data-handling terms distinct from inference.

> **Callout:** "They train on our data" and "they retain our data" are different claims with different controls. Conflating them in a security review costs you credibility. Training is about model weights; retention is about stored logs.

## Question 2: Retention

All three vendors retain API inputs/outputs for a limited window by default — commonly on the order of weeks — for abuse monitoring and debugging, with enterprise options to shorten it, including **zero-data-retention (ZDR)** arrangements for qualifying customers where requests are not persisted at all. Your job: know your policy's maximum tolerable retention per data class, get the vendor's commitment *in the contract*, and remember that your own logging (gateway logs, traces, eval datasets) is frequently the *longer-lived* copy — vendor ZDR means little if your own observability stack keeps prompts forever.

## Question 3: Residency and Question 4: Access

**Residency**: where inference happens and where any retained data rests. The cloud channels shine here — Bedrock and Vertex inherit region pinning from their platforms, which is often the deciding argument for consuming Claude or Gemini through a cloud reseller rather than direct APIs (regional guarantees on direct APIs have been narrower, though all vendors keep expanding options). EU-heavy enterprises feel this constraint first and hardest.

**Access**: SSO/SAML enforcement, SCIM provisioning, role-based admin, audit logs and compliance APIs on the product side; API-key hygiene and IAM-scoped credentials on the builder side; certifications (SOC 2-class reports, ISO-series, sector attestations) as the paperwork that compresses your review. All three vendors check these boxes at enterprise tier — differences are in maturity and granularity, so review the current trust documentation rather than assuming parity.

## Threats the Vendor Cannot Fix For You

A balanced view: most enterprise AI risk lives on *your* side of the API.

| Threat | Whose control | Mitigation pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Prompt injection via retrieved/tool content | Yours | Treat all retrieved text as untrusted; least-privilege tools; output filtering |
| Oversharing through RAG/AI search | Yours | Permission-inheriting retrieval; access audits before rollout |
| Secrets pasted into chats | Yours | DLP integration, policy, training — and governed seats so you can even see it |
| Vendor data breach / insider | Vendor's | Contracts, certifications, retention minimization |
| Model behavior drift | Shared | Pinned versions, eval regression gates |

> **Adoption pitfall:** Security teams sometimes respond to AI with a blanket ban — which reliably produces shadow AI with *zero* controls instead of governed usage with strong ones. The mature posture is a **data-classification matrix**: public and internal data broadly allowed on approved platforms, confidential data allowed with specific controls, regulated data gated case-by-case. Bans don't stop usage; they stop *visibility* of usage.

## Policy as Code

Here is the operational leap most organizations miss: vendor-configuration requirements can be **checked automatically**. Your policy says "no training on our data, retention ≤ 30 days, region pinning required." Every vendor/channel configuration either satisfies those predicates or does not — and a twenty-line function can render the verdict, run in CI against your config inventory, and page someone when procurement adds a tool that fails. That is exactly the challenge: a retention-policy checker that takes vendor configs and a policy dict and emits per-vendor verdicts with named violations, deterministically.

Compliance-as-code beats compliance-as-spreadsheet for the same reason evals beat vibes: it reruns for free, it never gets tired, and its complaints arrive *before* the audit instead of during it. Pair it with an annual review of each vendor's trust portal — policies genuinely change — and the four dreaded questions become a dashboard, not a meeting.

description.en: The four security-review questions plus your data classes, compiled into an automated compliance checker.

left.label: Ban AI tools until the risk is zero

right.label: Classify data, approve governed platforms, and check vendor configs as code

description.en: Bans produce shadow AI with zero controls; governance produces visible usage with strong ones.

explanation.en: Training and retention are different claims — the checker tests them as separate predicates.
Retention is compared against YOUR policy maximum, not the vendor's default.
Residency requirements become a boolean gate — no pinning, no pass.
Return every violation by name: audit findings, not just a red X.

description.en: Three vendor configs, one policy, named violations — compliance rendered as a rerunnable report instead of a quarterly meeting.
The governance loop: policy as code, contracts for what code can't enforce, and scheduled re-review because policies change.

instruction.en: Build a data-retention policy checker — compliance as code. Complete check(cfg, policy) returning a list of violation strings IN THIS ORDER: 'trains on data' if cfg['trains_on_customer_data'] is True; 'retention <days>><max>' (e.g. 'retention 90>30') if cfg['retention_days'] exceeds policy['max_retention_days']; 'no region pinning' if cfg['region_pinning'] is False. Then, for the three vendors in CONFIGS in order, print EXACTLY four lines: '<vendor>: compliant' when there are no violations or '<vendor>: <violations joined by "; ">' otherwise, followed by 'compliant=' plus the count of compliant vendors. This is the check that runs in CI so policy drift pages an engineer instead of surprising an auditor.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
CONFIGS = {
    "vendor_a": {"trains_on_customer_data": False, "retention_days": 30, "region_pinning": True},
    "vendor_b": {"trains_on_customer_data": False, "retention_days": 0, "region_pinning": False},
    "vendor_c": {"trains_on_customer_data": True, "retention_days": 90, "region_pinning": True},
}

POLICY = {"max_retention_days": 30, "allow_training": False, "require_region_pinning": True}

def check(cfg, policy):
    violations = []
    # TODO: append "trains on data" if the vendor trains on customer data
    # TODO: append "retention <days>><max>" if retention exceeds the policy max
    # TODO: append "no region pinning" if region_pinning is False
    return violations

# TODO: for each vendor in CONFIGS order, print "<vendor>: compliant"
#       or "<vendor>: <violations joined by '; '>"
# TODO: print "compliant=<count of vendors with zero violations>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
vendor_a: compliant
vendor_b: no region pinning
vendor_c: trains on data; retention 90>30
compliant=1

en: Check the three predicates in the stated order so violation lists are deterministic.
The retention string is f"retention {cfg['retention_days']}>{policy['max_retention_days']}" — no spaces around '>'.
Join violations with "; " and count compliant vendors with a counter incremented when the list is empty.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert check(CONFIGS["vendor_a"], POLICY) == []
assert check(CONFIGS["vendor_b"], POLICY) == ["no region pinning"]
assert check(CONFIGS["vendor_c"], POLICY) == ["trains on data", "retention 90>30"]
assert check({"trains_on_customer_data": True, "retention_days": 365, "region_pinning": False}, POLICY) == ["trains on data", "retention 365>30", "no region pinning"]

q.en: What training-data policy has the market converged on for enterprise-tier AI products?

en: All data trains the models unless users object monthly
No training on customer business data by default, backed contractually
Training happens but only on weekends
Only metadata is used for training

q.en: Why is conflating 'trains on our data' with 'retains our data' a credibility mistake in security reviews?

en: Both are always false, so neither matters
They are the same thing legally
Training concerns model weights while retention concerns stored logs — different risks with different controls

q.en: Which threat is primarily YOURS to mitigate rather than the vendor's?

en: A breach of the vendor's data centers
Prompt injection through retrieved documents feeding your RAG pipeline

q.en: Why do blanket bans on AI tools usually increase risk?

en: Vendors raise prices for banned organizations
Usage continues as shadow AI with zero visibility or controls instead of governed usage with strong ones
